title: Madeleine McCann: Full Timeline of the Case 🔍
segments: On May 3, 2007, three-year-old Madeleine McCann disappeared from apartment 5A of the Ocean Club resort in Praia da Luz, Portugal. Her parents, Kate and Gerry McCann, were dining with friends roughly 50 meters away. The case became one of the most documented missing-child investigations in modern history.

The McCanns and their friends — a group later called the Tapas Seven — had been checking on the children every 20 to 30 minutes. Kate discovered Madeleine missing at around 10 p.m. The window in the children's bedroom was open. The shutters were lifted.

Polícia Judiciária officers arrived within an hour. Initial responders did not seal the apartment. Hundreds of guests, journalists, and well-wishers passed through the room over the next 48 hours, contaminating any potential evidence.

The early Portuguese investigation focused on Robert Murat, a British expat living roughly 100 meters from the Ocean Club. He was named an arguido — a formal suspect — but no charges were brought, and a 2014 libel case in his favor produced a settlement worth over half a million pounds.

British police were initially observers. The Portuguese investigation, led by Gonçalo Amaral, considered the parents themselves as suspects in 2007. Cadaver dogs from South Yorkshire reportedly alerted in the apartment and on a hire car the McCanns rented weeks later.

Forensic samples taken by the Forensic Science Service in Birmingham were inconclusive. The lab reported the DNA could not be confirmed as Madeleine's. Portuguese prosecutors closed the case as arguidos against the McCanns in July 2008.

The McCanns mounted a worldwide campaign. They released photos showing Madeleine's distinctive right eye coloboma — a streak of brown extending from her pupil into the green iris. The image was distributed in over 35 countries.
Tips poured in from across Europe, Morocco, and beyond. None produced Madeleine. Some sightings were investigated for months and ruled out.

Here's where the case takes a turn. In 2011, Scotland Yard launched Operation Grange, a parallel review funded by the British Home Office. Detective Chief Inspector Andy Redwood led a team of more than 30 officers.

Operation Grange identified 600 persons of interest across multiple countries. They digitized phone records from cellular towers near Praia da Luz on the night of May 3, 2007. Roughly 100 numbers became priority leads.

By 2013, Operation Grange had reduced the suspect pool dramatically. The team focused on burglars active on Portugal's Algarve coast and on a possible link to a string of break-ins targeting British vacationers.
In 2017, Operation Grange's funding was cut, but a small team continued. The German federal police — the Bundeskriminalamt — entered the case after a tip about an inmate at a Kiel prison.

On June 3, 2020, German prosecutors publicly named Christian Brückner, a German national with a long criminal record in both Germany and Portugal. Prosecutor Hans Christian Wolters told the BBC he believed Madeleine was dead and that Brückner was responsible.

Brückner had lived in the Algarve from 1995 to 2007. He had a phone signal in Praia da Luz on the night Madeleine vanished, according to German prosecutors. He had multiple convictions for burglary and child sex offenses.
In 2019, a German court convicted Brückner of raping a 72-year-old American tourist in Praia da Luz in 2005. He was already serving time for unrelated offenses.
In 2024, Brückner stood trial in Braunschweig, Germany on charges related to other sexual assaults in Portugal — not Madeleine's case. He was acquitted on those specific charges in October 2024.
German prosecutors continue to investigate him as the prime suspect in Madeleine's disappearance. Wolters has said publicly that they have evidence but not enough to bring charges yet.

The Portuguese investigation, technically reopened in 2013, remains active. So does Operation Grange in the UK. As of 2025, the British Metropolitan Police had spent more than 13 million pounds on the case.

In May 2023, German and Portuguese investigators searched the Arade Reservoir near Silves, an area Brückner is known to have visited. Items recovered were sent for forensic analysis. Public results have been limited.
Brückner's prison release date is currently set for September 2025. German prosecutors have warned that without new charges he could leave Germany and become much harder to track.

Kate and Gerry McCann have continued to advocate publicly. They founded the Madeleine Fund to support the search and to help other missing children. They have never been recharged in any jurisdiction.
Madeleine would have turned 22 in May 2025. The age-progression image released by Scotland Yard remains one of the most distributed in missing-child history.
Critics of the early Portuguese handling note that crucial hours and forensic chances were lost. Defenders point to Operation Grange's methodical work as proof the case can still be solved.
If you have information about Madeleine McCann, you can contact Operation Grange directly through the Metropolitan Police. The case is open. The reward originally posted by Madeleine's family stands.
Subscribe to DarkFiles and hit the bell. Next week's case goes deeper into seven brothers and sisters who vanished outside Adelaide on a calm summer day.
